name: A Tour around Aviles -- circular route
name_original: Una vuelta por Avilés — ruta circular
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Avilés offer a diverse landscape, blending urban charm with natural features. The region is characterized by its extensive coastline, the Avilés estuary, and inland greenways that follow disused railway lines and river valleys. Cyclists can explore flat, accessible paths along the waterfront or venture into more challenging terrain with rolling hills and scenic coastal roads. This variety provides options for different skill levels, from leisurely rides to longer, more demanding adventures. Best touring cycling routes around Avilés The most popular touring cycling route is A Tour around Aviles -- circular route, a 7.4 miles (11.9 km) trail that takes about 1 hour to complete. This easy route explores the city's urban and green spaces, including paths along the estuary. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Muros de Nalón – Bridge over the Nalón River loop from Avilés, a difficult 73.3 miles (118.0 km) path. This route offers extensive coastal views and challenging climbs into the Asturian interior. Local touring cyclists also love the Muros de Nalón – Bridge over the Nalón River loop from Villalegre, a 63.4 miles (102.0 km) trail leading through coastal landscapes and river valleys, often completed in about 6 hours 30 minutes. Touring cycling around Avilés is defined by coastal paths, estuary routes, and inland greenways. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy family rides to difficult long-distance challenges. The routes in Avilés are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 80 reviews. More than 1300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Avilés's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Avilés?
A: There are over 80 touring cycling routes around Avilés, offering a wide range of experiences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 80 reviews.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Avilés?
A: Yes, Avilés offers several easy touring cycling routes perfect for beginners or families. For example, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048890844/a-tour-around-aviles-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;A Tour around Aviles -- circular route&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 7.4-mile (11.9 km) trail that explores the city's urban and green spaces, including paths along the estuary. The region's greenways, like the Mills Route in Illas and the Water Route in Corvera de Asturias, are also designed for tranquil, undemanding rides.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes around Avilés?
A: Yes, circular routes are available for touring cyclists in Avilés. A popular option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048890844/a-tour-around-aviles-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;A Tour around Aviles -- circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which is an easy 7.4-mile (11.9 km) loop exploring the city and its estuary.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see while cycling around Avilés?
A: Touring cycling routes in Avilés showcase diverse natural features. You can explore the scenic Asturian coastline, the Avilés estuary with its waterfront paths, and inland greenways that wind through lush forests and green meadows, often alongside rivers. Notable natural attractions include the dramatic cliffs and views from &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4016988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabo Peñas Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt; and the urban cove of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5039958" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa María del Mar Beach&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to explore on a touring cycling trip in Avilés?
A: Absolutely. Avilés boasts a well-preserved historic center with cobbled streets and traditional architecture. Along the estuary, you can see the modern &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353338" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Niemeyer Center&lt;/a&gt; and sculptures made from recycled materials. Other historical points of interest include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4404073" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Llumeres Iron Mine Ruins&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353338" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of St Thomas of Canterbury&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What scenic viewpoints are accessible by touring bike in the Avilés area?
A: The region around Avilés offers several scenic viewpoints. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4016988" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabo Peñas Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt; provides breathtaking views over the Cantabrian Sea. Another excellent spot for panoramic vistas is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5584287" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Peñona Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there challenging long-distance touring cycling routes in Avilés?
A: Yes, for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, Avilés offers difficult long-distance routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31858386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa María del Mar Beach – Work of art loop from Llaranes&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 33.2-mile (53.5 km) route with significant elevation changes. These routes often feature extensive coastal views and climbs into the Asturian interior.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Avilés?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Avilés is generally during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) months. During these periods, the weather is typically mild and pleasant, with less rain and comfortable temperatures for cycling. Summer can also be good, but it might be warmer and more crowded, especially along the coast.

Q: Are there options for parking near touring cycling routes in Avilés?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around Avilés, particularly near urban starting points or larger natural areas. Many routes begin or pass through towns and villages where public parking facilities can be found. It's advisable to check specific route details for parking recommendations.

Q: Is public transport available to access touring cycling routes in Avilés?
A: Avilés is well-connected by public transport, including local and regional bus services, and a train station. These options can help cyclists access various starting points for routes or return from longer tours. Some public transport services may accommodate bicycles, but it's always best to check their specific policies in advance.

Q: Are there places to eat or drink along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Avilés pass through or near towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and pubs. The historic center of Avilés itself offers numerous options, and coastal towns like Salinas are known for their dining establishments. Inland routes, particularly those following greenways, may have fewer options, so it's wise to plan accordingly.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Avilés?
A: While many natural areas in Asturias are dog-friendly, specific rules can vary for cycling routes, especially those passing through protected areas or private land. Generally, dogs on a leash are welcome on many greenways and less crowded paths. However, it's always recommended to check local regulations for each specific route to ensure a pleasant experience for both you and your dog.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Avilés?
A: The touring cycling routes in Avilés are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, from the scenic coastal paths and the unique Avilés estuary to the tranquil inland greenways. Cyclists appreciate the variety of routes catering to different skill levels and the blend of natural beauty with cultural landmarks.
tips: A cultural complex designed by the famous Brazilian architect Oscar Niemeyer and inaugurated in 2011. It consists of several cone-shaped buildings and a large central square. Inside there is a concert hall, a theater, several exhibition halls, a cinema and a library. The design of the Center is very characteristic with its organic curves that Oscar Niemeyer used in many of his other projects. In addition, it has several murals and sculptures designed by Niemeyer himself.

An 18th century hermitage on top of a hill called Cerro de la Magdalena. It offers panoramic views of the city and its surroundings. It is a popular pilgrimage site in Avilés, especially during the fiestas in honor of the Virgen de la Luz in September.

A public park located in the heart of the city. The park is named after Pedro Menéndez de Avilés, a famous Spanish explorer who founded the city of San Agustín in Florida. It was designed in the 19th century by the French landscape designer Eugène Belgrand, and was inaugurated in 1894. It is known for its beautiful gardens, large ponds, sculptures, fountains, and historical monuments. It also has a children's playground and hosts various cultural events throughout the year, including concerts, festivals and exhibitions.

San Nicolás de Bari attracts visitors from all over the world who come to admire its architecture and Gothic art. The church is dedicated to Saint Nicholas of Myra, also known as Saint Nicholas of Bari, who was a 4th-century bishop in what is now modern Turkey.
It was built in the Gothic style between the 13th and 14th centuries. One of its most outstanding features is its impressive collection of altarpieces, dating from the 15th and 16th centuries. They feature intricate carvings and elaborate paintings, and are considered some of the best examples of Gothic art in Asturias.

The cathedral image of the Church of St. Thomas of Canterbury evokes the desires of modernity, urban expansion and demographic increase that the old fishing village lived at the end of the nineteenth century, crowning the bourgeois expansion of the new city.

At the end of the nineteenth century the small Romanesque temple of Sabugo, the old fishing district of Avilés, had become too small to accommodate all its parishioners. The whole city mobilized to build a new temple that would be a symbol of the progress that Avilés was then experiencing: the City Council, the local bourgeoisie, even the Avilesian emigrants in Cuba did not hesitate to contribute their money and their effort to make the project a reality.
Symphonic organ: Thanks to the contribution of individuals, companies and foundations, the church has since 2010 a symphonic organ by the builder Federico Acitores, the second largest in Asturias, after the Basilica of Covadonga.

Lignum Crucis: Since in 1953 he received, through the intercession of Bishop Lauzurica, a fragment of the relic of Lignum Crucis that is preserved in the Monastery of Santo Toribio de Liébana (Cantabria), this has become the treasure and symbol of the Brotherhood of Our Lady of Solitude and of the Holy True Cross, who venerates the Virgin since ancient times in her invocation of Our Lady of Solitude. The wood splinter will be shown in a chestnut wood niche, the work of cabinetmaker Paco Cueva.
https://aviles.es/en/iglesia-de-santo-tom%C3%A1s-de-canterbury

Old fishermen's neighborhood with the old chapel, not to be confused with the new cathedral of the same name built in 1903 when the old became too small. The old square is still surrounded by medieval columned merchant streets, now a popular restaurant area
